chain_of_thought: Tools are unavailable (connection refused), so I cannot decompile or inspect native/Java code and must reason only from the abstract crash description.

The crash occurs in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum during certificate parsing routines (Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT, Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT, CERT_Load), called from a JNI method jGetKeyUsage. This strongly suggests internal ASN.1/X.509 parsing logic, likely implemented in libUbikeyJni.so, operating on attacker-supplied certificate data.

Fuzz harness entry fuzz_one_input indicates arbitrary inputs are being fed into this stack; in a real app, those inputs would plausibly come from external, untrusted certificates (smart card, server, NFC, etc.). ASN.1 parsing is historically fragile, and functions like Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum often iterate child nodes, calculate indices, or length fields based on structure metadata; off-by-one or bounds bugs here can easily become out-of-bounds reads/writes or invalid memory access.

The termination reason is only the top function name (Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum) without an explicit abort reason (e.g., sanitizer, assert). It could be a crash or an internal error return. However, the presence of a dedicated fuzzer and the function naming suggest this crash is a result of malformed ASN.1 structures triggering undefined behaviour rather than a deliberate abort on bad input.

Without code, I cannot prove the specific operation (e.g., memcpy, index, pointer arithmetic). But the function naming and call stack identify classic vulnerability-prone parsing code that acts directly on attacker-controlled structured data. Since fuzzing detected a crash, it is likely a memory safety issue in this parsing path, not a benign error path. No evidence indicates the harness is passing impossible values that production could never hit; fuzz_one_input is likely just replacing real external certificates.

Given that this is core parsing of ASN.1 certificate structures used for key usage extraction, and malformed inputs can be provided by an attacker, this should be treated as a likely genuine vulnerability, with at least DoS and probably memory corruption potential. Lack of sanitizer frames in the trace prevents exact CWE assignment but standard issues are out-of-bounds read/write during child index computation or length miscalculation (CWE-787/125/190).

Because I cannot see the exact code or confirm the operation causing the crash, confidence must be moderate, not maximal, and CWE list may be approximate. I will classify it as vulnerable with medium-high confidence and high severity (memory corruption in certificate parsing reachable from JNI).
reasons: Crash occurs in ASN.1/X.509 certificate parsing routine Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum called via CERT_Load from JNI, a classic location for memory safety bugs under malformed inputs.
Fuzz harness provides arbitrary structured input analogous to attacker-controlled certificates, so malformed external data can realistically reach this code in production.
Lack of allocator/sanitizer frames suggests a direct fault in application-level parsing logic rather than a controlled abort, pointing toward unsafe index/length handling in ASN.1 child computation.

evidence.note: Top crashing frame while iterating ASN.1 structure children during certificate parsing, indicating fault in index/length logic on structured input.
Loads and parses certificate data before converting to X509 structures; likely processes attacker-provided certificate bytes.
JNI entry used to obtain key usage from a certificate, reachable from Java and conceptually supplied by external inputs.

recommendations: Manually audit Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum and surrounding ASN.1 parsing routines for index and length validation, especially loops over child elements and pointer arithmetic.
Add robust bounds checking on ASN.1 child counts and lengths before accessing buffers; use size_t-safe helpers and reject structures with inconsistent lengths instead of trusting encoded values.
Run targeted fuzzing directly on CERT_Load/Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT with ASan/UBSan enabled to reproduce and pinpoint the exact failing operation for patching.
assumptions: Certificate/ASN.1 data processed by CERT_Load can be influenced by untrusted external sources in the real application.
The fuzzer-induced crash reflects the same parsing path used in production, not a special harness-only testing mode.
The termination at Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum is due to an invalid memory access or similar unsafe operation, not an intentional safe abort.

chain_of_thought: Tools are unavailable (connection refused), so I must infer from crash metadata only.

Top crashing frame is __memcpy, immediately below is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, then Get_DER_Child, then Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT, then CERT_Load, then the JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage, then fuzz_one_input/main.

This looks like a typical ASN.1 / X.509 certificate parsing pipeline inside libUbikeyJni.so. Decode_DER_ASN1_ST likely parses a DER-encoded ASN.1 structure and uses memcpy to copy fields, lengths, or child elements into internal buffers.

A memcpy crash usually indicates out-of-bounds read/write, mis-sized buffer, or invalid pointer. In certificate/ASN.1 parsing code, these are often driven by length fields from the encoded input. The fuzzer input is the DER blob, passed through JNI as some byte array or buffer; key usage is a certificate extension, so the parser is expected to process attacker-provided certs (e.g., from smart card / token / network).

No Java call graph is provided, but JNI bridge name jGetKeyUsage and CERT_Load in the stack imply that Java passes certificate data or a reference to it to native code, which then calls CERT_Load to parse it. fuzz_one_input is explicitly the harness entry, so the same native path is reachable by arbitrary fuzzed bytes (here treated as attacker-controlled).

Because __memcpy is the termination point, and there is no evidence of defensive bounds checking, it is reasonable to infer an unsafe memcpy or incorrect length calculation in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST or below. ASN.1 length fields are highly attacker-controlled and historically error-prone. This is classic memory-corruption territory (CWE-787 / CWE-119) rather than a deliberate abort.

Nothing in the description suggests this is just harness misuse (e.g., passing NULL where app never does). The stack shows a realistic parsing path (CERT_Load → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → Get_DER_Child → Decode_DER_ASN1_ST → memcpy). These are functions one would expect in production code, not just in a harness.

Thus, this is likely a genuine vulnerability: out-of-bounds memcpy / heap/stack buffer overflow or invalid pointer due to malformed DER. Exploitability could be high (potential RCE) but at least a strong DoS and possible memory corruption.

Without code, I cannot pinpoint exact argument tainting, but the data obviously originates from untrusted DER and there is no suggestion of strict pre-validation. Confidence slightly below 0.9 due to missing decompilation.
Severity: high, because of probable memory corruption via memcpy on attacker-controlled lengths in a certificate parser reachable through JNI.
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST while parsing ASN.1/X.509 structures, indicating likely out-of-bounds memory access
Data path from fuzz_one_input through CERT_Load and JNI jGetKeyUsage implies attacker-controlled DER/certificate bytes reach the parser without clear bounds enforcement
ASN.1 length-driven memcpy in certificate parsing is a known high-risk pattern for memory corruption and the stack trace matches that pattern

chain_of_thought: Tools are unreachable (connection refused), so I must reason only from the textual crash entry and my domain knowledge.

The top native frame is __memcpy, called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST → CERT_Load → JNI method Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage. That strongly suggests a memcpy inside ASN.1/DER certificate parsing.

DER/ASN.1 decoding typically copies variable-length fields (e.g., extensions like KeyUsage) based on length values encoded in the certificate. In this fuzzing context, fuzz_one_input almost certainly mutates a certificate or DER blob passed to CERT_Load/JNI.

Without code, I can’t prove exactly which argument to memcpy is invalid (src, dst, or len). However, crashes inside memcpy during parsing of untrusted DER are very often due to insufficient bounds checks on the encoded length (OOB read/write) rather than a harness-only misuse, because the natural API contract is that the parser must safely handle arbitrary bytes.

Is this attacker-controllable in the real app? KeyUsage is derived from certificates, which in identity/PKI apps often originate from external sources (smartcards, files, remote servers). Even if the specific app normally gets certificates from a trusted source, the fuzz harness indicates the library is designed to parse arbitrary DER without prior validation by Java.

No evidence that the JNI boundary or Java side constrains input size or validates the certificate structure before passing it to CERT_Load. The presence of a dedicated Decode_DER_ASN1_ST function implies native code owns the parsing and thus must do validation internally.

The crash is not clearly a harness-only artifact like passing NULL into an API that production code never does; instead, it is exactly the kind of malformed-data parsing crash that would occur if an attacker could supply a certificate with inconsistent length fields.
Given this, I infer an out-of-bounds access or similar memory safety issue in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST or CERT_Load when handling DER lengths, which is a genuine vulnerability pattern (CWE-787/125).

Severity is at least high because a memcpy-based OOB in a certificate parser can be exploitable for code execution or at minimum allows denial-of-service; but without seeing the exact code (stack protections, exact direction of OOB) I cannot justify "critical" with very high confidence.
Confidence is moderate (~0.7): pattern is strong and realistic, but lack of code visibility and call graph details limit certainty.
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy during DER/ASN.1 decoding (Decode_DER_ASN1_ST → CERT_Load), a classic site for length/bounds bugs
Input to the parser is controlled by the fuzzer and plausibly by external certificates in real use, with no evidence of prior length validation in Java/JNI
Pattern strongly suggests an out-of-bounds memory access due to trusting encoded length fields, which is a genuine memory-safety vulnerability
recommendations: Review and harden Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and CERT_Load to validate all DER/ASN.1 length fields against actual buffer sizes before memcpy/memmove operations
Add robust bounds checks and centralized helpers for reading ASN.1 length-prefixed fields, returning errors instead of copying on invalid lengths
Enable and rerun with ASan/UBSan and targeted fuzzing on CERT_Load/Decode_DER_ASN1_ST with minimized reproducers to pinpoint the exact failing memcpy
assumptions: CERT_Load/Decode_DER_ASN1_ST accept DER/ASN.1 data derived from attacker-controlled certificates in at least some deployment scenarios
The crash in __memcpy is due to an invalid length or pointer caused by parsing logic, not by an artificial harness-only misuse like deliberately passing NULL pointers
